In response to https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1476#issuecomment-180611390
> My thinking is that if a feature is present in the environment, any package that has that among its options should use it, even if that means selecting an older version.

That is the behavior I intuitively expected during my first steps with conda, so I vote +1 for making this the default. On the other hand, users should have the option to override it, as the following use case demonstrates: Suppose a package comes in two variants, a pure Python one (without any features), and a faster compiled one (with the appropriate compiler feature). When the compiler feature is being tracked, `conda install` should pick the second variant by default, but give the user the option to choose the first. This is especially useful during development, when the newest version only exists in the pure Python form.

Another important consideration is to prevent recipe designers from accidentally forgetting a feature. I'm wondering if it makes sense to implement an `auto` keyword for features:
```
build:
  features: - auto
```
If `auto` is present, `conda build` would read all `track_features:` specifications from direct dependencies (specifically, from the precise variants of these dependencies in the build environment) and insert them automatically into the present recipe.

> If that is the case, that is not a valid URI

Agreed.

According to my understanding, conda has a fast version resolution algorithm that sometimes (actually, often) misses features, and a correct one that is too slow by a factor of 10. @asmeurer is trying to speed-up the correct algorithm in order to phase-out the old code.

In my Windows builds, I work around this issue by appending `.vc11` to the version string whenever a package defines `features: -vc11`. Dependencies are specified as `foo *.vc11` so that non-feature versions are no longer considered in package resolution, even by the old algorithm. To make this nicer, I introduced jinja variables for these strings, see for example [hdf5/mata.yaml](https://github.com/ilastik/ilastik-build-conda/blob/toolset-config/hdf5/meta.yaml). This technique can readily be transformed to other matching problems. However, it depends on the pending PRs https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/1601 and https://github.com/conda/conda-build/pull/575.

If you read the [source](https://github.com/hibtc/cpymad/blob/71f1ff319e4a68c8e55e64696136394f6717d37a/cpymad/madx.py#L296) or [documentation](http://hibtc.github.io/cpymad/cpymad/madx.html#cpymad.madx.Madx.set_value) or if you had basic knowledge of MAD-X itself (in cpymad you can do anything you can do in MAD-X using `.input()` as I have stated several times...), you could figure out a way on your own in 5 minutes.

For reference, here are the most relevant ways to achieve this. Assume, you have MAD-X elements, defined like this:

```
k1_qp1 = 1;
qp1: quadrupole, l=1, k1:=k1_qp1;
qp2: quadrupole, l=1, k1=1;
mul: multipole, knl={1, 1, 1};  ! a list
```

in MAD-X, you can change their values as follows:

```
K1_QP1 = 2;
qp2->k1 = 2;            ! also works for qp1
mul, knl={2, 2, 2};     ! this is the only method that works for lists
                        ! (but it also works for qp1,qp2 as well!)
```

where the syntax at the bottom is the most generic, i.e. can be used to modify the values for all cases, and the syntax in the middle can be used to modify either of the first two elements.

Now in cpymad, you have several options:

- you can use `.input()` to directly execute commands that would work in MAD-X itself, so if you know the MAD-X command, you can do in cpymad, e.g.:

```python
m.input('k1_qp1 = 2;')
m.input('qp2->k1 = 2;')
m.input('mul, knl={2, 2, 2}')
```

- there is a convenience method that covers the first two cases:

```python
m.set_value('k1_qp1', 2)
m.set_value('qp2->k1', 2)
```

- the global parameters are also accessible via the `Madx.globals` attribute, so the first case could be written as:

```python
m.globals['k1_qp1']
```

- Also note that I have been planning for some time to create special support for this to cover all three cases in a more uniform and convenient manner, but this remains not implemented as of now, see #25, but I guess you did not even read through the other issues before dropping a new one.
Cheers!

The Java side throws an exception, because it cannot determine what is the intended output data type of your `LookupTransformer` step - you are using `int` data type for dictionary values, but `float` data type for the default value.

To avoid this exception, you need to use the same data type for both.

Promoting dictionary values from `int` to `float` (pay attention to the added decimal separator `.`):
```Python
LookupTransformer({"a": 1., "b": 2., "c": 3.}, 0.)
```

Demoting the default value from `float` to `int` (pay attention to the removed decimal separator `.`):
```Python
LookupTransformer({"a": 1, "b": 2, "c": 3}, 0)
```

I would like to reorganize your transformer class to make it more generic. Specifically, I want to move the initialization of the "base dict" object outside of this transformer class.
```Python
class LookupTransformer(TransformerMixin):

  def __init__(self, base_dict, default_value = 0.0):
    self.base_dict = base_dict
    self.default_value = default_value

  def fit(self, X, y = None):
    return self

  def transform(self, X):
    transform_dict = defaultdict(lambda: self.default_value)
    transform_dict.update(self.base_dict)
    return np.array(X.apply(lambda x: transform_dict[x]))
```

You could then construct an instance of `LookupTransformer` based on a CSV file in local filesystem like this:
```Python
base_dict = pandas.read_csv(lookupfile).set_index('key').to_dict()['value']
lookup = LookupTransformer(base_dict)
```

Alternatively, you could define the key-value mapping manually:
```Python
base_dict = {"red" : "no", "yellow" : "no", "green" : "yes"}
lookup = LookupTransformer(base_dict, "no")
```

> When Lori and I were discussing ways to reorganize the directory structure, I brought up this pmag.py and ipmag.py merge possibility. Lori's perspective (which she convinced me of at the time) is that it makes sense to keep them separate with pmag.py containing the functions that mostly do a single thing and ipmag.py containing functions that bring pmag.py functions together to do more of a work flow (e.g. do a bootstrap reversal test and have options to save the associated output). In this way, the ipmag.py functions are similar to the command line programs that are built on top of pmag.py functions, but are built to be used without sys.argv calls, etc. The current split between the modules isn't entirely consistent with this design conception, but it largely is. I like the idea behind adding a `pulp_smash.tests.docker.cli.utils` module. If we can share code, why not? However, I'm not so sure about the implementation of things as it stands. The benefit behind having such a module is that its code can be shared - but no sharing is done right now.

Making re-usable code is *hard*. Really, I know! In my experience, a good time to introduce re-usable code is when there really are multiple uses for a given piece of code. This has the obvious benefit of ensuring that you've correctly designed that bit of code. But it also has the side benefit of ensuring that changes are better isolated in the commit history, and therefore can be rolled back or understood after-the-fact.

How about adding the new utilities directly into module `pulp_smash.tests.docker.cli.test_copy` as private functions, and then extracting them out at a later point when the re-use is needed?

We write code as listed above in many tests. But curiously, it's entirely unnecessary. Authentication credentials are already present in API clients, and the `get_auth` function is used to randomly assign either HTTP BASIC or certificate-based credentials to the API client, the idea being that randomly using either kind of credentials may give better test coverage than always using HTTP BASIC credentials.

I'd like to at least rename `get_auth` to `get_random_auth`, which better reflects what's going on. If posible, I'd love to drop many of the `api_client.request_kwargs['auth'] = get_auth()` lines of code from the code base, so that others who read our test code don't get tricked into thinking that this is somehow necessary.

I'm not sure how to solve this issue, but it deserves attention before Pulp Smash becomes a framework.

3. Further compartmentalizes the sections of the k8s deployment for easier readability.

With that being said, your celerybeat.pid file is the process ID, not a timestamp. I've never seen a pid file contain a timestamp before.

If you're creating a Django project that integrates with Celery, you'll want to just deploy your entire project on each worker node. Each node will have a copy of Django, but it obviously wouldn't be running under WSGI. Each Celery worker can then use the models provided in `django-celery-results` and the same database connection provided by Django. I'm not sure what you would gain by using the pure database backend on each worker and only `django-celery-results` on your running WSGI Django app.

If we find this kind of whitespace stripping to be generally desirable, I would say we should handle it in a parameter callback -- those are very easy to share and chain on the fly.
However, and this is important, `ParamType.convert()` happens _before_ `Parameter.callback`.
That means that an argument or option with `callback=strip_whitespace`, `type=click.INT` will insist on not seeing whitespace around the int (because that enforcement comes earlier).

If we really want to strip whitespace prior to other kinds of type enforcement (e.x. allow spaces around a UUID), it might be best to define `WithoutWhitespace` as a type that accepts, as one of its parameters, another `ParamType` to which it will chain all of its methods.
Otherwise, even if you make it a mixin class, you'll be defining new types all over the place.
That's somewhat risky because they actually have some [irritating subtleties](http://click.pocoo.org/6/api/#click.ParamType).

Thanks for explaining:

> I find it quite "dirty" to have my plumbing scripts accessing os.environ all around.

Totally agree with you there. (Aside: Even for writing shell scripts I think it's a good practice to grab all the env variables up front, and give them a default value if they haven't been defined. This way a reader of the code can quickly see all the environment variables that are used, and you lessen the risk of using an unset environment variable incorrectly.)

> ... the old days where you had no idea what a shell script function needed to work

Agree here too. I also definitely appreciate the appeal of having all inputs defined in a function signature, unlike with traditional shell scripts where the env variable usages are scattered throughout.

**Question / Example**
_Question:_
One thing I was hoping you would mention though is why you'd rather not reference the environment variables in the shell script that _calls_ your Python Fire CLI. Why is this?

_Example, illustrating the question_
You said you'd like to use Fire like this:
```
export HOST=mydesiredhost
./machine_manager ping -- --use_environment
./machine_manager shutdown -- --use_environment
# No need to pass --host=mydesiredhost on each command
```
But you can already use it like this:
```
export HOST=mydesiredhost
./machine_manager ping --host=$HOST
./machine_manager shutdown --host=$HOST
# There's no need to pass --host=mydesiredhost on each command
```

So why do you prefer the former? One reason I can think of is it that there can be a large number of environment variables, and so the latter approach could become messy. A second reason I can think of is that you now have to keep the list of args in sync between the Python script and the shell script, which can be a maintenance burden. Let me know if there's a different reason you were thinking of (or maybe you didn't know the latter approach was possible and it _is_ good enough :)).

----------------

Lastly, here are some concerns to consider for implementation:

- Python Fire supports functions that take **kwargs and *varargs.
  - If someone used the --use_environment flag with a function that accepted **kwargs, would we pass in the _whole_ environment?!
- Python Fire supports calling functions on members returned by other functions.
  - Multiple functions might accept variables with the same name.
  - When using command line arguments, we can use the argument order.
  - How would we resolve this case when using --use_environment?

1.  I just found out you can catch sys.exit calls with `except SystemExit`. Maybe there's a way to have Fire raise a SystemExit error that includes the fire-specific information, thereby giving status codes in the default case while still allowing the client to catch and examine the error information if they choose.

2. Currently `Fire` has 0 documented keyword arguments (and 2 undocumented keyword args that you shouldn't rely on, since they might change or be removed). So, I would be hesitant to add a keyword argument at this point for a small feature like this. [That said, there are things for which it will be appropriate to add keyword args in the future (e.g. you should be able to directly pass argv to Fire rather than having it use sys.argv), and we'll have to think through these carefully in order to keep a clean API going forward.]

```
class WechatAuth(object):
    """微信公众平台 OAuth 网页授权 """

    API_BASE_URL = 'https://api.weixin.qq.com/'
    OAUTH_BASE_URL = 'https://open.weixin.qq.com/connect/'

    def __init__(self, app_id, secret):
        """

        :param app_id: 微信公众号 app_id
        :param secret: 微信公众号 secret
        :param redirect_uri: OAuth2 redirect URI
        :param scope: 可选，微信公众号 OAuth2 scope，默认为 ``snsapi_base``
        :param state: 可选，微信公众号 OAuth2 state
        """
        self.app_id = app_id
        self.secret = secret
        self._http = requests.Session()

    def _request(self, method, url_or_endpoint, **kwargs):
        if not url_or_endpoint.startswith(('http://', 'https://')):
            url = '{base}{endpoint}'.format(
                base=self.API_BASE_URL,
                endpoint=url_or_endpoint
            )
        else:
            url = url_or_endpoint

        if isinstance(kwargs.get('data', ''), dict):
            body = json.dumps(kwargs['data'], ensure_ascii=False)
            body = body.encode('utf-8')
            kwargs['data'] = body

        res = self._http.request(
            method=method,
            url=url,
            **kwargs
        )
        try:
            res.raise_for_status()
        except requests.RequestException as reqe:
            raise WechatOAuthException(
                errcode=None,
                errmsg=None,
                client=self,
                request=reqe.request,
                response=reqe.response
            )
        result = json.loads(res.content.decode('utf-8', 'ignore'), strict=False)

        if 'errcode' in result and result['errcode'] != 0:
            errcode = result['errcode']
            errmsg = result['errmsg']
            raise WechatOAuthException(
                errcode,
                errmsg,
                client=self,
                request=res.request,
                response=res
            )

        return result

    def _get(self, url, **kwargs):
        return self._request(
            method='get',
            url_or_endpoint=url,
            **kwargs
        )

    def fetch_session_key(self, code):
        """获取 session_key

        :param code: 授权完成跳转回来后 URL 中的 code 参数
        :return: JSON 数据包
        """
        res = self._get(
            'sns/jscode2session',
            params={
                'appid': self.app_id,
                'secret': self.secret,
                'js_code': code,
                'grant_type': 'authorization_code'
            }
        )
        self.open_id = res.get('openid')
        self.session_key = res.get('session_key')
        self.union_id = res.get('unionid')
        return res
```

Just a brief user feedback: I've been made aware of this PR in the gitter channel and use the embedded interpreter in a toy project: https://github.com/fuchsto/dnants/blob/master/gos/src/client.cc
It's for students to play around with state machines. It's an ant colony simulation written in C++, the ants' behavior is implemented in Python as state transition function. It is called about (30fps x 50 ants x 3 colonies) = 4500 times per second and I didn't see any negative effect on FPS compared to native C++ code.
Briefly put, I am impressed.
However, performance dropped drastically when I used `py::eval<py::eval_statements>` instead of `py::eval<py::eval_expr>`. I don't know if this is to be expected, but it should be documented.
Same with the RAII-style `py::scoped_interpreter guard`. It's a good solution but it took me the better part of two hours for debugging because it's not documented and, if not used, just leads to a miraculous segfault.

>> Now we have:
>> 
>> 1. Calculate computed values, fill in default values, and check for missing required fields.
>> 2. Run the before[insert/update] callbacks.
> 
> That's not true, the actual flow is:
> 
> 1. Enqueue for computation or fill default
> 2. Check required fields empty from point 1
> 3. Compute computations enqueued from point 1
> 4. Run callbacks

I just meant to indicate that everything in 1 happens before 2. The specific ordering of items within 1 was not relevant to my point -- the issue is when the callbacks run relative to 2 and 3 in your list.

> I think moving callbacks before point 2 is wrong for two reasons:

Sorry, I thought you agreed with moving callbacks before 2 (though not necessarily before 3) based on your comment [here](https://github.com/web2py/pydal/issues/480#issuecomment-313641975).

> if your callbacks relies on computed or fields that are required, it won't work

We should think about required fields (at least those that aren't also computed fields) and computed fields separately. First, regarding required fields, while it is true that the callbacks will fail if they depend on a required field that is missing, that is not an argument for checking required fields first, as the failure will happen in either case. On the other hand, if we allow the callbacks to run first, at least they will have an opportunity to fill in missing required fields, thereby preventing an error.

Regarding computed fields, I suppose we could go either way on that. On the one hand, the callbacks might rely on values from the computed fields. On the other hand, the callbacks might alter some of the values used by the computed fields. I would lean toward supporting the latter case, simply as a matter of backward compatibility (i.e., that's the way it was before this change).

> you will have to double check empty values

Sure, but if that's what it takes to get the proper workflow, we should do it. In fact, that's how it was done previously.

> Also, the example given is pointless since it can be achieved putting a default on the involved fields.

Sure, but the bigger issue is one of backward compatibility. This change [broke](https://groups.google.com/d/msg/web2py/w4A9Wo2f6As/ZF1Ax0ZnBAAJ) at least one person's working code. It's not clear what benefit we get from checking required fields after the callbacks, so I would rather not break existing code for the sake of that change.

> Plus, if you want to make things to fields before computation, why you don't just put the modification code on computation?

Are you suggesting that within the code for a computed field we should promote the mutation of *other fields*? The `compute` attribute is associated with a specific field and expected to return a value for that field -- using it to generate external side effects would be unexpected and could cause confusion.

> My final point: the best option I can thing to have a correct flow for everyone here is to add another callback method, for example before_validation and put it between point 1 and 2.

I agree we could add another hook, though I'm not sure it would be worth the complexity. What was wrong with the old approach, though? Were there particular use cases that motivated the switch to the new workflow?

I agree that we should have passed the stream from the start, but give
yourself some credit.  The ZMQStream API was created very early on,
before anyone else had done anything even close to this.  It is very
hard to guess in advance how things will be used.  That the API has
remained as stable as it has is amazing.

I am a little more willing to add the new functions under some
reasonable name.  Some people will find them useful and they do serve
a purpose that the lambda/closure trick does not: their presence would
encourage people to think about how they can write their code to pass
streams around like this.  The lambda/closure trick is also a bit
subtle and many people would resort to other ways of passing the
stream around, such as storing it in a global location, which is
definitely not good.

Go ahead and make the final decision on this one, but I am +1 on
adding them under a new name.

Looks good so far! Cool that https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/issues/164#issuecomment-84996815 seems to have been free of errors :)

> Or it's supposed to work that slowly?

It gets quite a bit slower, yes. I don't see a way to speed it up. The idea is that it would still converge more quickly in terms of wall time, but that depends on your data.

> Can you comment on the difference between the Keras implementation and your implementation?

Hmm, they've got two modes: One is using the running batch mean and running batch std deviation (i.e., `axis=0`) to normalize the features, that's what my implementation does for `deterministic=True` to be able to compute the validation error. The other mode is doing something that doesn't have anything to do with the paper, it normalizes the last feature dimension (i.e., `axis=-1`) of each individual training example. They don't have a mode that does what's written in the paper they cite, but the first mode might work similarly well (that would need some experiments).

> It seems that Keras does not use the default update trick but saves the updates with self.update?

Well, the only problem with the default update trick is that it will update the running mean and std deviation if and only if running with `deterministic=False`, assuming that you're doing this for training, and will use the running mean and std deviation instead of the batch-wise mean and std deviation if and only if running with `deterministic=True`. There's currently no way to say "use and update the running mean and std deviation during training" (the first mode in Keras), and there's no way to say "use the batch-wise mean and std deviation, but don't care about updating the running mean and std deviation".

> Could something similar be added to lasagne?

We could add some explicit update mechanism, but that would complicate usage somewhat. It would need to be coupled with `layers.get_output()` or `Layer.get_output_for()`, because that's where we have the input expressions for each layer. So we'd either need to change the API to have `get_output()` return an update dictionary along with the output expression -- that's strongly against our fifth design goal -- or to optionally pass an update dictionary that can be modified by the layers in the `get_output()` call.
I don't see much of an advantage against the current solution of merging the update dictionary with the output expression, though. This, too, could be controlled via some keyword argument to `get_output()` / `get_output_for()`, i.e., we could have `BatchNormLayer` react on some argument like `update_norms` that overrides the assumption based on `deterministic=True` or `deterministic=False`. And it relieves the user from having to obtain a second update dictionary to keep and pass on to `theano.function`.

So unless we find a use case that doesn't work well with default updates, I guess we could leave the API as it is and merge the batch normalization layer as it is. I'll make a PR some time soon to move the discussion there!

> If I am not wrong at least when the correct symbolic input variables are supplied when constructing the InputLayer instances, this should be doable with memoization or manual caching.

You're still wrong, because the graph does not only depend on the input variables, but also on the keyword arguments (e.g., `deterministic`). If it wasn't for dropout or batch normalization, we'd construct the expressions on the go while creating the layer instances. Whenever you deviate from the scheme of building a function that creates the expression (e.g., by using `get_output` outside of `get_output_for`), you run into trouble, much in the same way you run into trouble when you call `.eval()` on a Theano expression to do some numpy acrobatics with the values. This is a fundamental problem that cannot be solved by memoization.

> With memoization you could have a suppressable warning whenever the node is caching multiple graphs

No, it couldn't guard you against what you were doing.

> To have access to tensors can be very useful in many cases, e.g., to debug with tag.test_value

Let's stick with the "Lasagne is to Theano what Theano is to numpy" analogy for a while. `tag.test_value` associates a numpy array with a Theano variable so you can debug what an expression would do for that value. The `get_output` cache would be similar: It would associate a Theano expression with a Lasagne layer so you can debug what a Layer would do for that expression.

> to perform computations based on the symbolic output shape of a layer

Sticking to the analogy above, that would be equivalent to writing a Theano expression that uses (the shape of) `tag.test_value`. If you do this, your Theano expression becomes dependent on the test value you associated with the input variable. Similarly, if you incorporate the cached symbolic expression into a Layer, it will make your Layer dependent on the input variable you associated with the input layer, and possibly also to the keyword arguments you passed to `get_output_for`.

Maybe allowing symbolic expressions in `ReshapeLayer` and `TransposedConv2DLayer` is a slippery slope.

> My point is that here one needs to know and understand very well what's going on under the hood to realize that he is doing something potentially dangerous.

Yes. It's the same for Theano, but usually you will end up with an error sooner or later if you try `.eval()` to be able to use numpy within your Theano expression. If you know what's going on under the hood, you will not attempt this, and if you don't know what's going on, you will get an error. I don't know how we could reach a similar warning mechanism for Lasagne.

Do you know how Blocks solves this? How do they get the training and testing expressions (enabling/disabling dropout and batch normalization)?

@ottomata I don't think I'm looking for a "grand merge". I think, instead, I'm trying to think about how to make sure there isn't wasted effort from this point forward.

For example, pykafka and kafka-python provided varying degrees of production support for Kafka during its "stumbling infant" period in open source, and that's fine -- natural exploration/competition is what open source does best.

But now we have a stable librdkafka, and relatively mature pure Python projects. We have an opportunity to do things like: a) evaluate the relative pro's and con's of each API; b) benchmark their real-world performance, and c) think about long-term maintenance.

There are also practical concerns of the limited maintainer hours for open source contributors and the migration path for existing users of each project.

I think what the community wants in the long-term is something like what we have with Redis, Cassandra and Elasticsearch -- there is a "clear" choice for the Python module/API to use (redis-py, cassandra-driver, and elasticsearch-py respectively), even though there are historical projects that have supported older versions of each API, but which now point people to the right place. (e.g. pycassa now points people to cassandra-driver).

I don't think we need to go as far as trying to model everything that Spark is providing (like a context object that you can play with in a Python REPL).  The infrastructure of Storm is too different from Spark to allow for that sort of experimentation where you take some data and apply a bunch of functions to it that magically get executed on the cluster.  With Storm your tuples are always coming out of a spout and then just getting fired through the topology.

Anyway, I really like the idea of just requiring that people have Storm installed.  It simplifies a lot of things.  For example, we could just reuse `storm shell` to submit our Python DSL topologies if we did (although we would probably want to provide a less verbose wrapper around it).

Writing tuples to databases in storm would be a side-effect of a bolt in your topology.

You'd have your spout reading data, sending it some number of bolt, and one of those bolts would do the correct `INSERT` or `UPDATE` statements, depending on your database schema. Storm provides "at least once" guarantee of tuples, which means that your database writes should be prepared for sometimes attempting to re-save a tuple that had already been processed.

The idea is similar to the `RedisWordCountBolt` in the examples https://github.com/Parsely/streamparse/blob/master/examples/redis/src/bolts.py

Does that answer your question?

`gettempdir` retrieves a global temporary directory which is created if it doesn't exist, but just returned if it does. This means that if you have two models using this metric on a single machine, the tempdir file might be shared, which makes bad things happen, because the filenames aren't unique inside the directory, meaning things get overwritten.

Hmm, couple of things:

* `from_email='"customer@example.com" <support@company.com>'` should work just fine. That is, from_email is a *single* string, with a single email address (mailbox) whose display-name is "customer@example.com" and whose addr-spec is "support@company.com". (And that's a good approach if you're trying to change how the "From" is displayed in the recipient's email client.)

* Looking more closely, I see that Anymail is imposing an additional requirement on `from_email` that Django core email doesn't: Django would allow `from_email='first@example.com, second@example.com'`—still a single string (not a Python list), but with multiple, comma-separated emails in it. And it would pass that string, more-or-less intact, on to the underlying email backend as the From header.

I can look at making that work in Anymail. But the problem is still going to be that most ESP send APIs don't allow multiple from addresses. And they tend to be pretty picky about allowing header overrides of standard fields like From. So it'll probably just be a different error getting raised.

If you do not define a role for your function, SAM will create one per function for you. While this seems extraneous, it actually scopes each function individually. So now you can mutate the roles for each function without affected the collection. I do not recommend created one role for every function, due to blast radius concerns and to keep each function least privileged. 

For each integration to the Lambda Function, we create a Lambda Permission. While this is probably over the top and we can possible reduce this, we are following this model until we have a use-case to merge them into one.

As I read your comment on #4025, it looks like the issue you were experiencing was because of line `<IfModule mod_rewrite.c="">` in the configuration. Even though the internal configuration parser of Apache2 is very permissive, unfortunately the Augeas library which Certbot uses for configuration parsing is not, and we don't have the tools to affect the behavior of it.

The `<IfModule mod_rewrite.c="">` is actually a syntax error based on Apache 2.4 documentation: https://httpd.apache.org/docs/2.4/mod/core.html#ifmodule

Unfortunately the error message we get from the Augeas library as presented in the original issue seems to point to a line of *merged* configuration, and using that we're unable to pinpoint the exact line in a file where the error occured.

I'm sad to say that all I can suggest here is going through your configuration and trying to find the erroneous line manually.

> The plan being to take the hostname or remove /directory from the initial server URL, take the path from the account URL, and add it to the end of the server URL? I worry that this hardcodes assumptions about the layout of the ACME server which isn't guaranteed.

I share your reservations about this approach. It's likely over-fitting to the way Let's Encrypt has set up its ACME directory URL and corresponding endpoints.

> This happens because in acme.client.BackwardsCompatibleClientV2 we're using the ACMEv2 client, but we POST to the URL taken from the registration on disk which is an ACMEv1 URL. 

Perhaps one potential solution would be to POST the `newAccount` endpoint using the ACMEv2 ["only return existing" feature](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-acme-acme-12#section-7.3.1) to learn the ACMEv2 account URL for the given legacy keypair. It would be an extra round trip but you would be guaranteed to get the correct URL to update for the account and wouldn't have to do any URL manipulation specific to Let's Encrypt.

Please understand that the error is not from Curator, but from Elasticsearch.  Curator is an index selection wrapper on plain old Elasticsearch API calls.  If you had made the API call directly (e.g. command-line, or the Console in Kibana), the result would have been the exact same.

With that said, the error _may_ (or may not) indicate something else going on:
```
org.elasticsearch.repositories.RepositoryException: [repo] concurrent modification of the index-N file, expected current generation [1290], actual current generation [1293] - possibly due to simultaneous snapshot deletion requests
```
You're trying to snapshot to a file that is being modified _while_ you are snapshotting.  This should not be possible.  What kind of repository is it? Do you have multiple clusters accessing the same repository?  [Curator will not allow a `snapshot_delete` to happen while a snapshot is `IN_PROGRESS`](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v5.4.1/curator/actions.py#L971-L976), as it tests to see if something is going on, but that state is only within a single cluster (if you had multiples accessing the same repository).

I can't say for sure what's happening in your cluster, but I can say with much certainty that it isn't likely to be Curator that is causing the problem.

Note that imageio already provides measures to make Pillow work (it overloads the seek operation so that when Pillow uses seek for the current position, it's a no-op). The primary reason we can't support `\[` is that it is valid Markdown/CommonMark for an escaped bracket.

[As written](https://talk.commonmark.org/t/mathematics-extension/457/18?u=rgbkrk) by @jgm:

> ...the problem with that syntax is that `\[` and `\(` already have clearly defined meanings: they are escaped `[` and `(` characters, and it is very important that there be a way to escape these characters in certain circumstances.

We all want to support mathematics typesetting. For maintainers and operators of notebook server deployments, we can't support extensions to the syntax that are unambiguous. This does mean having to wait it out for others to solve within CommonMark or contributing there.

Supporting grammars would be a really nice convenience for developers. Most ASR UIs are not dictation based, but specified by a command-based grammar (ie. UIs for visual impairment, telephony, handsfree applications). You want to constrain the vocabulary in certain contexts to avoid spurious detections. While this could be handled by the client side, this would require testing some threshold criteria, searching through the top N probabilities, or implementing some sort of Viterbi search. I would like to use DeepSpeech for [idear](https://github.com/OpenASR/idear), but this is currently difficult due to the dependence on a fixed grammar. HMMs still work better, even if the detection accuracy is lower.

The use of several training files has nothing to do with the loss diverging. You're changing the training data, which can cause the training process to diverge. You need to tweak your hyperparameters until you find something that works. As a first step I suggest increasing the batch size or lowering the learning rate if your batch size is constrained by the available memory.

And I think other people have had problems with using the "other" split of Common Voice, as it can be way off from the transcriptions. So you might want to stick with the "valid" part, or switch to a speech corpus that's known to work with a set of hyperparameters, like LibriSpeech (see bin/run-librivox.sh).

I train my models from scratch, and here is my process:

I record wav's  and add the corresponding sentences in a textfile (vocabulaire.txt)
I use this textfile to create lm, trie, and the present vocab

1/ As you see, I add sentences when I create wavs, but you could, too, add texts, without corresponding wavs.  This bigger textfile could be used to create your lm and trie files.
Lm and binaries act like probabilities (ex : I have 4 words ! What is their best order, regarding to lm)

The bigger the lm, the best words order in sentence (in the facts!)
But the more material (wavs/sentences), the best the model (depending of the deep structure of course.)

I am closing that as WONTFIX for now, since we have no short or mid-term of having support for ARMv6 hardware. However, for posterity, @elpimous has been able to build for ARMv8 with CUDA support.

He also has been able to successfully get ARMv6 binaries to work on ARMv8 (Ubuntu for Tegra) through multilib:
- sudo dpkg --add-architecture armhf
- sudo apt-get update
- sudo apt-get install libsox2:armhf libstdc++6:armhf

Then one just has to download and extract the ARMv6 `native_client.tar.xz` and the following should work:
```
$ LD_LIBRARY_PATH=$(pwd):/lib/arm-linux-gnueabihf:/usr/lib/arm-linux-gnueabihf:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH ./deepspeech
```

Functions: _lower_case_ name. Ex: 
`do_something(...)`

Global variables: _UPPER_CASE_ name. Ex: 
`set(SOMEVARIABLE "...")`

Local variables: _lower_case_ name. Ex: 
`set(some_variable "...")`

Control statements: _lower_case_ name without repeat the condition in the closing brackets. Ex:
```
if(condition)
  ...
else() # not repeat condition
  ...
endif() # not repeat condition
```

Operators: _UPPER_CASE_ name. Ex: 
`if(condition STREQUAL "")`

Directives and/or extra options:  _UPPER_CASE_ name. Ex: 
`do_something(... USE_THIS)`

An real example:

```
function(set_platform system_name)
  if(${system_name} MATCHES "Darwin")
    set(PLATFORM "darwin")
  elseif(${system_name} MATCHES "Linux")
    set(PLATFORM "linux")
  else()
    set(PLATFORM "")
  endif()
endmacro()

cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.0)
set_platform(${CMAKE_SYSTEM_NAME})
```

@fmassa, thanks for your feedback. I understand your reservations: this proposal does not extend existing functionality, but only provides a nicer syntax. As for advantages of the proposed API over your code snippet:
* I find chaining is slightly conceptually simpler than nesting
* In more complex cases (i.e. validation over several similar-but-different transformation pipelines) it'd be more concise and readable.

As for efficiency, the code I provided was a first approximation; I guess creating new object could be avoided in most cases and we could end up with creating fewer objects compared to nesting `Compose`s (we could prepend/append to a `Compose.transforms` whenever possible).

Nevertheless, I agree it is for the community to decide if this proposal adds any value.

That helps. These errors:
```
ERROR - The extended key usage extension is missing from the client certificate. Session client identity unavailable.
```
```
cryptography.x509.extensions.ExtensionNotFound: No <ObjectIdentifier(oid=2.5.29.37, name=extendedKeyUsage)> extension was found
```
```
kmip.core.exceptions.PermissionDenied: The extended key usage extension is missing from the client certificate. Session client identity unavailable.
```

indicate that your client certificate does not contain the X.509 ``extendedKeyUsage`` extension. That is one of the extensions that can be used to determine if your client certificate can be used to actually authenticate the client's identity (instead of just being used to secure the connection).

To **temporarily** get around this problem, you can set ``enable_tls_client_auth`` in the server configuration to ``False``. This will allow the server to skip the check for the ``extendedKeyUsage`` extension. For more information on this, see the [documentation](https://pykmip.readthedocs.io/en/latest/faq.html#how-does-the-pykmip-server-handle-client-identity-and-authentication).

If you're going to be using PyKMIP long term, I'd recommend regenerating your certificates to included the missing extension. That way, they are properly marked and follow the X.509 spec. Of course, many people don't bother so it's ultimately up to you.

I'm thinking possibly for the license switch, we're best off if the `LICENSE` file contains just the Apache 2.0 license, and we include a `licenses/` folder with the BSD license. I think we can treat all work subsequent to the license change as a derivative work of the old version that is licensed under Apache 2.0. Hopefully it won't be terribly confusing, and we can explain the license situation in the `README`.

The one thing I'm slightly concerned about is if this will cause headaches down the line as people may have to include multiple copyright notices just for incorporating `dateutil` into their product. It's *probably* fine, but there isn't a *huge* advantage to switching to Apache 2.0, particularly if we add a notice under `CONTRIBUTING.md` that explicitly says that all contributions will be assumed to be licensed under BSD.

A third option is to add a `Contributing.md` that says all contributions going forward will be assumed to be dual-licensed under BSD and Apache 2.0. We'll leave the main license of the project under BSD 3-clause because that's the most straightforward. If everyone on the AUTHORS list agrees to relicense under Apache 2.0, we can switch Apache 2.0 to the main license in the LICENSE file.

> Also, what exactly is the point of pre-processing these like this? Both the tokens and the separators are available to the parser, and it frequently re-combines them as necessary. Why is this case special such that we should violate this separation of concerns?

After `timelex.split` the parser _doesn't_ recombine tokens.  It occasionally examines neighboring tokens for contextual inference, but assumes that each individual token is "right".  e.g. all of the "is this a valid XYZ?" checking is based on "is this token included in the dictionary for XYZ?"  Which is why in the status quo "US/Eastern" and "A.M." can never be correctly identified by the parser.  We _could_ try to re-combine these within `parse`, but after looking at it I determined it's much simpler to avoid splitting them in the first place.

> I am, generally speaking, wary of doing parsing in the lexer. 

At least in the short term I agree.  We have zero tests in test_internals for the tokenization.  If at any point we _do_ agree to change anything there, we should have a test suite for it to avoid regressions.

That said, the tokenization is likely the part of the process that is the most amenable to being formally specified.  This might also be a good candidate for behavioral-style testing+fuzzing.

> What's the reason these would not just be a couple new pytest tests? The detailed console output?

There are a number of parameters you might want to tweak when running a benchmark, like how many iterations to run, or which database to use. It seems unnecessarily complicated to pass those parameters in through pytest.

But (additionally) running the benchmark in pytest is valuable to make sure that the benchmark is kept up to date with any trinity API changes. I'm imagining a test as simple as:

```py
def test_benchmarking_is_stable():
    run_benchmark(iterations=3)
    # yay, didn't crash
```

> The benchmarking aspect has nothing to do with the runtime performance but is really all about ensuring certain test scenarios still work.

The test is to make sure the benchmark works. The benchmarking _is_ about runtime performance.

Alerta is event-driven (ie. the events are alerts) so an external source is required to check to see if **no** heartbeat has been received for a heartbeat origin. To achieve this, I have modified the `alerta` command-line tool to add an argument called `--alert` to the the `heartbeats` sub-command. Previously, the `alerta heartbeats` command would just list all the heartbeats received. Following alerta/python-alerta@10ae853b5dff666141be6291eae9c9372716c809, the command-line tool will now generate an alert if the timeout is exceeded or the heartbeat latency (ie. receive time - create time) is greater than 2000ms. **Example** In this example, a heartbeat has not been received within the 30 second timeout interval. ``` $ alerta heartbeats --alert ``` So, the above command will result in the following output: ``` ORIGIN TAGS CREATED LATENCY TIMEOUT SINCE web01 linux, london 2015/09/03 00:22:35 25ms 30s *0:00:34 aed61d8e-ac77-4da8-8822-aafa1d3647b2 (normal -> major) ``` And the generated alert would look like this in the web console... All you need to do now is schedule this to run as often as you like in `cron`.

Hi,

Thanks for your contribution. 

I should probably elaborate more on this in the documentations, but your modifications cannot be used in the current format because they are implemented on the auto-generated `.js` file. 

## Explanation

Our JavaScript code is written in TypeScript ([see the docs](https://neutronx.github.io/django-markdownx/js/js.html#compatibility)) using ECMA 2016 in files [markdownx.ts](https://github.com/neutronX/django-markdownx/blob/master/static-src/markdownx/js/markdownx.ts) and [utils.ts](https://github.com/neutronX/django-markdownx/blob/master/static-src/markdownx/js/utils.ts) under `./static-src/markdownx/`. These are then trans-compiled onto the ECMA 5 standard to produce `.js`/`.min.js` files. Any changes made directly to the auto-generated files will thus be lost in the next compilation. The trans-compilation process is a semi-automated process that is launched through the [developers' environment](https://neutronx.github.io/django-markdownx/contribution.html).

## Solution

There are two ways we can go about this:

- I can implement the changes myself and commit them to the repository.

- You can do it yourself in the `.ts` file, then either compile and commit them, or alternatively if you don't want to go through the hassle, commit them and I will do the compilation work myself. 

The advantage of the latter option is that the contribution will be registered in your own name.

> Some servers break with @ in UIDs.

I guess you're referring to Issue #526. Well, that is a server bug, no? If the server can't deal with UIDs in a form that is recommended by the RFC…

> Why? What do you gain from this?

* More informative directory listings are preserved (in case server uses RFC recommendation for UID)
* Information about where the entry was created is stored in the UID, which may be useful for analysing issues.
* vdirsyncer's repair functionality not touching entries that don't need to be touched and therefore being less invasive (less chance of messing things up) and running quicker
* Other clients not losing their UID-based state; my phone's common correspondent list was reset after doing a repair to fix a single duplicate UID (DAVdroid as client) which is quite bothersome, so this did affect me.

Also, we don't how do the myriad of clients react when they see all the UIDs have changed. I guess they think all existing entries are gone and an equal number of new ones appeared. > [@ is] a totally safe character in URL paths in theory.

I think I agree with you here, https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc3986#section-2.2 says:

> If data for a URI component would conflict with a reserved character's purpose as a delimiter, then the conflicting data must be percent-encoded before the URI is formed.

Given that @ would not conflict when in the path part of a URL, it does not need to be encoded.

After a discussion with @lukasgraf and @phgross we have decided to slightly change the implementation and how it behaves by default. Instead of calling the upgrade `deferred` and omitting it by default we will now call it `deferrable` and install it by default.

advantages:
- prevent that upgrades are not run and lie around indefinitely, especially with our current tooling around `update_deployment`.
- easier workflow for small deployments where the deferred/deferrable upgrade will be be run together with the other upgrades (transactionality, simpler path). no extra action has to be taken when upgrading those. for larger deployments we always take extra care with the upgrade-path, we will still have the option to omit deferred upgrades then.

@patrickleet Hey all I was having an issue with this too. There's an SSL issue that doesn't seem surmountable, however a "hack" to fix it is to patch `sputnik` to disable SSL verification temporarily.

If you edit `session.py` in your Sputnik library folder you can disable SSL verification by amending your imports as follows. For me this file was located at:

`/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/site-packages/sputnik/session.py`

`import ssl` and
`from urllib.request import Request, build_opener, HTTPRedirectHandler, HTTPCookieProcessor, HTTPSHandler` (adding `HTTPSHandler`)

Then in `init` modify the code to read as follows before the `self.opener = build_opener`...line

```
        ctx = ssl.create_default_context()
        ctx.check_hostname = False
        ctx.verify_mode = ssl.CERT_NONE

        self.opener = build_opener(
            HTTPSHandler(context=ctx),
            HTTPRedirectHandler(),
            HTTPCookieProcessor(self.cookie_jar))
```

You may want to revert after for safety.

Je ne suis pas sûr de comprendre suffisamment bien quel est le problème plus général.

Je peux inférer deux objectifs potentiels :

1. Réduire la charge administrative sur les mainteneurs.
2. Accélérer la livraison des PR.

Et j'entends deux solutions potentielles exprimées :

A. Externaliser le rebase sur les contributeurs.
B. Réduire la taille des PR.

Avec cette PR proposant de pousser vers A.

Malheureusement, j'ai le sentiment que l'intensité de ces deux problèmes sont inversement proportionnelles (i.e. je crois qu'externaliser le rebase ralentit la livraison), ce qui s'explique par le faible niveau moyen en Git des contributeurs. Le coût d'une opération comme rebase est très élevé pour le contributeur moyen (au point qu'il y a un risque de perte de code, d'introduction d'erreur ou d'abandon de contribution) alors qu'il est plusieurs ordres de grandeur plus faible pour les mainteneurs.

Je suis convaincu que la solution A est trop risquée car elle augmente démesurément le coût de la contribution. Je crois qu'on ne devrait l'appliquer que sur les contributeurs les plus expérimentés.

Et cela fait longtemps que l'on tente systématiquement de mettre en place la solution B. Malheureusement, vu le coût administratif fixe du changelog, nous avons des _incentives_ perverses. Je ne suis pas certain que les contributeurs comme l'IPP, par exemple, aient été particulièrement convaincus que cela accélérait la livraison de leurs PR.

Ma conclusion est que les deux solutions sont pertinentes, mais qu'elles ne pourront pas être effectivement implémentées en modifiant le template de PR. Inversement, je vois des risques majeurs à demander la mise en œuvre de A sans accompagnement personnalisé. Je considère donc que le RoI du changement proposé est <0.

Ma dernière expérience d'accompagnement m'a montré que même le template actuel de PR est ambigu et le jeu de contraintes pas évident à comprendre pour un nouveau contributeur.

- - - -

Je vois deux autres solutions possibles aux problèmes listés plus haut :

C. Augmenter progressivement le nombre de contraintes appliquées aux contributeurs, en créant l'équivalent d'un « budget de relecture » que les mainteneurs passent à examiner des contributions. Il faut construire cela de manière très attentive pour bien montrer qu'il s'agit de faire monter en compétence et non de refuser l'accompagnement.
D. Abandonner la lisibilité de l'historique et basculer vers un système de merge autorisant le merge upstream.

Je ne suis pas convaincu par l'une ou l'autre  :(

Enfin, si l'un des problèmes non explicités est les conflits rencontrés par les mainteneurs au rebase, il existe des solutions techniques pour limiter ces effets que je serai heureux de présenter si besoin.

So here's what I've got on the spaces issue:

You can pass subprocess.Popen() either a single string containing the entire command and options ( "ls -al /etc" ) or you can pass the command and every option as a list ( ['ls', '-al', '/etc'] ). The list method is preferred because it is resilient to spaces or other field separators in options and command paths. For example, subprocess.Popen("c:\program files\java\jdk1.8\bin\jarsigner.exe -verify c:\path\to\apk") will not work, because the space in "Program Files" isn't automagically escaped.

I thought the solution to this was to re-write all of the delegator.run() calls to be list-based calls instead of string-based calls. This works on Windows! Yay! Unfortunately, it doesn't work on Mac or Linux hosts that I tested. Digging further, I found that Delegator explicitly sets the 'Shell' flag from subprocess.Popen() to 'True'. Having this set to 'True' while also passing in a list-based command string apparently results in ambiguous, undefined behavior on a non-Windows OS- in our use case, subprocess.Popen() was interpreting the very first list argument as the entire command string, which obviously wouldn't work.

I was able to get Mac and Linux calls working by manually setting Shell='False' for the subprocess.Popen() call - this is recommended best practice, I believe, because Shell='True' allows shell metacharacters to be interpreted. Unfortunately, I hit another snag here - Delegator doesn't seem to allow you to explicitly set the Shell=False flag for its subprocess.Popen() calls, it sets it to 'True' and doesn't expose any way of changing that property.

For now, I worked up a quick re-write that uses subprocess.Popen() manually - this works on all three operating systems with python 3.3+. However, it's not as pretty or convenient as having all the helper methods that Delegator provides. If you think it makes sense, I could write up a really light Delegator-esque helper class in helpers.py that exposes much of the functionality that this project was using from Delegator (mostly stdout and stderr reading).

What do you think? Worth rewriting to tear out Delegator? Or should I look for some work-around that keeps Delegator in the project? Maybe a PR to their project that allows manually setting of the 'Shell' argument to subprocess.Popen()?

I think you are asking about how to choose the `scales` argument to `cwt`?  

We don't have much CWT documentation currently, but what values are appropriate is going to depend on the signal you are analyzing.  There is one concrete example given here:
https://github.com/PyWavelets/pywt/blob/580d79d9440ec0f4f936892e39c79ad13a8fd33b/demo/cwt_analysis.py
The scales are closely related to the underlying frequency: see the function `pywt.scale2frequency` which is used to generate the `frequencies` array that is returned by the CWT.

The `cwt` function in PyWavelets is similar to the [CWT implementation in older versions of matlab](https://www.mathworks.com/help/wavelet/ref/cwtold.html). The documentation on scales at that link would apply equally to the CWT in PyWavelets.

The mload script works because you are converting the types implicitly by using `df.to_csv` (which must convert all columns to strings in order for it to write that to a file).  So in either case you have to convert the data types to compatible Teradata data types.  For example, if you used `df.to_csv` to write a common-delimiter file (with header) it would operate just like mload when you use the command-line interface to load it `giraffez mload ...` (but that would be a really roundabout way to do that, this was just to help illustrate what is happening when you use `df.to_csv`).

A couple of the core principles of giraffez is to be fast/efficient and promote data quality (correctness), so automatically converting floats to strings is not something that it currently will do.  There might be a compelling reason to do so, but libraries like numpy/pandas or even just Python itself make it really easy to handle these types, so it is often more logical to convert the values only in situations where they need to be converted (rather than incur the computational cost of checking it everytime for everyone).  For instance, you could rewrite your script like this:  

```Python
def coerce_floats(df):
    """Coerce numpy floats to Python strings"""
    float_types = ('float16', 'float32', 'float64')
    for name in df.columns.tolist():
        if df[name].dtype in float_types:
            df[name] = df[name].values.astype(str)


with giraffez.MLoad("DBC.TestTable") as mload:
    mload.columns = df.columns.tolist()
    coerce_floats(df)
    for row in df.values.tolist():
       mload.load_row(row)
    exit_code = mload.finish()
    print("finished load with code {0}".format(exit_code))
    if exit_code == 0:
       mload.cleanup()
```

Pandas DataFrame types use numpy arrays/types so it gets to use the great type conversion features of numpy.  As you can see above it is easy to create a function like `coerce_floats` that runs only once and directs the DataFrame to convert columns of a particular type to any other type (so you could add say numpy integers to that as well if you wanted to auto convert them).

With that said, I do think at a minimum that the error message should be better, and in fact the newest version of giraffez that should be releasing very soon, will have a much better error message indicating how you can fix your script (saying something indicating the mismatch in types and what is expected).

Ok, so being able to define multiple environments and choosing between them is one thing and having some user defined parameters/context is another. In addition there are two parts to the latter. So:

* User should be able to specify arbitrary parameters and they should be available to them through context
* User should be able to do pre-processing on those parameters, for example to do things like establishing database connections
* User should be able to select between multiple environments specified above

So I was only solving first two here. Current issues/questions.

* Should there be environment definition for what parameters can be specified? What abstraction should it belong to? 
* Which abstraction environment callback that creates eg connections will belong too?

So I feel former can live in pipeline *and* probably it can also somehow get it from solids. Eg when remaking SQL solids, I felt solids should have been able to specify "I require sql engine at particular variable in context", not use some implicit one. The latter should be outside a pipeline, so that you can have different environments created for eg local and prod.

So this PR has latter, but not the former. I didn't have time to finish the former, so I think you should merge this one (unless there are bigger issues) and add definitions at later stage.

I might be able to send a PR later, but what I did for now was to subclass Eventbrite and implement a get_all_event_attendees function that cycles through the pagination. This has poor guarantees for completeness, but it's better than completely missing out on any page > 1

````
class MyEventbrite(Eventbrite):
    def get_event_attendees(self, event_id, status=None,
                            changed_since=None, page=1):
        """
        Returns a paginated response with a key of attendees, containing a
        list of attendee.

        GET /events/:id/attendees/
        """
        data = {}
        if status:  # TODO - check the types of valid status
            data['status'] = status
        if changed_since:
            data['changed_since'] = changed_since
        data['page'] = page
        return self.get("/events/{0}/attendees/".format(event_id), data=data)

    def get_all_event_attendees(self, event_id, status=None,
                                changed_since=None):
        """
        Returns a full list of attendees.

        TODO: figure out how to use the 'continuation' field properly
        """
        page = 1
        attendees = []
        while True:
            r = self.get_event_attendees(event_id, status,
                                         changed_since, page=page)
            attendees.extend(r['attendees'])
            if r['pagination']['page_count'] <= page:
                break
            page += 1
        return attendees
````

@ialhashim, Most reference models for ImageNet (including DenseNet) perform 1/32 down-sampling followed by a global-pooling and a classification layer. Thus, the smallest possible `min_size` is 32; it is OK to create a DenseNet with `input_shape=(64, 64, 3)` if you change `min_size=64` in `_obtain_input_shape`.

The reason why `min_size` is set to 221, is a sort of convention. Usually, researchers handling ImageNet expect a 7x7 last feature map when a 224x224 input image is fed into a model. The 221 is the minimum value to make the last feature map as 7x7.

The inspector gives you a lot of information, including:
- The types of observables being checked
- The properties of each observable which are checked
- The values which the properties are compared against
- The comparison operators used in the comparison expressions
- The observation operators in use
- Any qualifiers in use in the pattern

Here is an example pattern, modified slightly from one of the test cases:
```
[foo:bar.xyz=1 and bar:foo not > 33] repeats 12 times
    or ([baz:bar issubset '1234'] followedby [baz:quux not like 'a_cd'])
```
The inspector returns a 3-tuple consisting of comparison data, observation operators, and qualifiers.  The most complex data is the comparison data.  Below is the comparison data which would be currently be returned for the above pattern:
```python
{'bar': [(['foo'], 'NOT >', '33')],
 'baz': [(['bar'], 'ISSUBSET', "'1234'"), (['quux'], 'NOT LIKE', "'a_cd'")],
 'foo': [(['bar', 'xyz'], '=', '1')]}
```
The outermost structure is: `{observable_type: list_of_comparisons}`.  So all comparisons for a particular observable type are grouped together.  In the above pattern, three observable types are in use, so the map has three entries: `bar`, `baz`, and `foo`.  `baz` is used in two comparisons, so there are two elements in its list.  The rest have only one.

The structure of each element of each list is: `(path_components, operator, value)`.  `path_components` is itself a list, since the inspector splits up each path into its components, to make it easier for users to identify them.  Most paths in this example have one component, but the first path has two (`bar.xyz`), so its corresponding list has length two.  The operator is a string copied from the comparison expression (but uppercased, for uniformity), and likewise for the value.  Quotes and the like are not stripped from a value, since they can assist in determining its type.

Observation operators are given as a set of strings.  Each operator in use is included:
```python
{'OR', 'FOLLOWEDBY'}
```
Finally, each qualifier in use is also given as a set of strings:
```python
{'REPEATS 12 TIMES'}
```
So I think the information described in your first bullet is there.  The information from the second bullet might be there, depending on what you're looking for.  "Observation expression" encompasses a lot of inner structure.  I think the components of that structure are basically there.

Here is a usage example I wrote, for a different task.  It is intended to determine whether a pattern consists of a single ipv4 address equality comparison:
```python
def is_ipv4_equals_pattern(pattern):
    """
    Determines whether the given pattern is of the form
        [ipv4:value = <some_address>]
    """
    results = pattern.inspect()
    return not results.observation_ops \
        and not results.qualifiers \
        and len(results.comparisons) == 1 \
        and "ipv4-addr" in results.comparisons \
        and len(results.comparisons["ipv4-addr"]) == 1 \
        and results.comparisons["ipv4-addr"][0][0] == ["value"] \
        and results.comparisons["ipv4-addr"][0][1] in ("=", "==")
```
The things it checks are, in order:
- There are no observation operators
- There are no qualifiers
- Only one observable type is in use
- That observable type is "ipv4-addr"
- That observable type is involved in only one comparison expression
- The observable path for that comparison expression is just "value"
- The the operator in use for that comparison expression is one of the equality operators

So that's a lot of checks, but I think it shows that you can get a lot of specificity from the data provided.  You could probably think of even more that could be included, but I think the more structure and complexity there is, the more complicated the resulting checks can be.  I just wanted to determine whether the pattern was a simple equality comparison of an IP address, but that took seven separate tests.  It could grow even larger.  A balance probably needs to be struck, between flexibility and usability.

@dan-blanchard 

> Would there be any objections to adding pip as an explicit dependency for pex?

I think it'd be great if we could avoid that for now just due to precedent/historical reasons, but wouldn't be opposed in the longer term if a proposal materialized that thoughtfully motivated the case.

> One thing I've noticed with this PR is that the wheels it picks don't always match the ABI tags they should.

yeah, this is a semi-known wart with current pex that is particularly relevant for shops without isolated indexes. pex differs from (pre-8.0) pip et al because fundamentally you can build a pex on one host and run it on another which may or may not share the same abi compatibility. I think to solve this, we probably would want to plumb an explicit argument or other config for constraining the resolve to only allowable flags with some sensible defaults. the runtime resolve checking should probably also detect compatible abis and error early if they fail to resolve - which could additionally pave the way for multi-abi pexes (e.g. one pex with both `m` and `mu` wheels that could exec on ucs2 *and* ucs4 systems and use the right deps accordingly). feel free to file an issue for this one if you have time.

> It feels like all the fetching/downloading stuff pex is doing could just piggyback on pip ...

yes, totally. we've effectively been waiting (roughly >a year now) on the change/set of changes you mentioned (patricklaw is also a pants/pex contributor) to land in `pip` to create a path for us to move to a pluggable resolver model that would let us slot pip directly into pex for resolution. this would allow us to deprecate the bespoke pex resolver in favor of pip's while also providing a backwards-compatible upgrade path. it's something we want to do, but is relatively invasive and at odds with other priorities - so if you had any interest in picking that work up, it'd be most welcome.

```
items = [(read_cache, vt.cache_key, vt.current_results_dir if self.cache_target_dirs else None)
             for vt in vts]
    res = self.context.subproc_map(call_use_cached_files, items)

The difference between using safe_rmtree andf safe_mkdir is that [safe_mkdir is not symlink safe](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/issues/4137) and silently converts the symlink into a directory. This is mostly silently okay but in the case where a user CTRL-C or some other interruption, they will get deterministic failures when the setup files try to unlink `current` only to find it is now a real dir.

There is a repro in the first bug here: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/issues/4051

These issues was actually my motivation for cleaning the results_dir for invalid tasks - I was tired of getting pinged for this issue 3-4 times a month :)

I removed almost every use of safe_mkdir(dir, clean=True) from the cache module except for the one you saw with my comment.  It was the only place that was operating over the canonical hashed result_dir (combined with some possible complexity around doing it in another way that I can't remember).

import aiohttp

VALIDATE_CERT = os.environ.get("RADIXDLT_CORE_VALIDATE_CERT", "no") == "yes"

async def async_http_get(session, url, timeout=10):
    with async_timeout.timeout(timeout):
        async with session.get(url) as response:
            assert response.status == 200
            return await response.text()

class CoreSystemCollector(object):
    URL = "{0}/api/system".format(BASE_URL)

    def __init__(self):
        self._loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
        self._session = aiohttp.ClientSession(loop=self._loop, connector=aiohttp.TCPConnector(verify_ssl=VALIDATE_CERT))
        self._async_task = None

    def async_refresh(self):
        try:
            self._async_task.cancel()  # cancel previous requests if any
        except AttributeError:
            pass  # 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'cancel'
        self._async_task = self._loop.create_task(async_http_get(self._session, self.URL))

    def collect(self):  # pylint: disable=no-self-use
        if self._async_task is None:
            self.async_refresh()  # backward compatibility

        self._loop.run_until_complete(self._async_task)
        if self._async_task.exception() is None:
            metrics = json.loads(self._async_task.result())
            yield CounterMetricFamily('radixdlt_system_memory_max',
                                      'Maximum memory available for the radixdlt core on this node',
                                      value=metrics["memory"]["max"])
        else:
            logging.error("failed to scrape metics from the core", exc_info=True)

It's already possible to add multiple images by calling `extra.append(pytest_html.extras.image(...))` multiple times. The assets will be unique because we already use an index for the extras. For me, just your CSS change of `clear: right;` gives the same results with the following hookwrapper:

```python
import base64

import pytest


@pytest.mark.hookwrapper
def pytest_runtest_makereport(item, call):
    pytest_html = item.config.pluginmanager.getplugin('html')
    outcome = yield
    report = outcome.get_result()
    extra = getattr(report, 'extra', [])
    if report.when == 'call':
        for image in ['image1.png', 'image2.png']:
            with open(image, 'rb') as f:
                encoded = base64.b64encode(f.read()).decode('utf-8')
                extra.append(pytest_html.extras.image(encoded))
        report.extra = extra
```

Everything else seems unnecessary. What's not ideal is that the height of the log is not extended to the full height of the multiple images. Also, if we had more than two images the report would become quite unreadable due to all the whitespace. I think it might actually be worth holding off and presenting the multiple images as a gallery. > The global registry issue is such that if you have two parts of the same application that have different demands then you're at an impasse.

No, one or both parts could still specify the `default` function to use, which would overwrite the built-in singledispatched one when the encoder is instantiated. And keep in mind that all *existing* code either doing this already or is subclassing `JSONEncoder` (which would also overwrite the singledispatched method).

> The code you're proposing will make that the status quo.

Incorrect - this PR preserves backwards compatibility with both subclassing, and with custom `default` arguments.

> There are many types that have more than one "obvious" encoding and libraries can and do disagree.

Sure, that's why I'm not prescribing any way of doing so. And end users still be able to control this, either by re-registering their own "correct" encoding function, or by subclassing `JSONEncoder`, or passing in a `default` function. This PR simply makes it easier for frameworks to help their users.

> In some cases, you may need some part of your program to behave a bit differently to accommodate a third party protocol.

Yes. And in all *existing* code, this is accomplished by subclassing and/or use of `default`, which this PR still supports in a backwards compatible way. For all future code, that functionality remains fully supported, useful, and usable.

> Consider also that many people are going to have both the json and simplejson modules installed and most libraries are only going to import and register handlers for one of them.

I will accept this criticism, however, I think it's a little weird to be using both libraries. Also, consider the fact that you can overwrite the `default` methods in both `json` and `simplejson`:

```python
@singledispatchmethod
def default(self, obj):
    raise TypeError('Object of type %s is not JSON serializable' % obj.__class__.__name__)

json.JSONEncoder.default = default
simplejson.JSONEncoder.default = default

@default.register(datetime)
def _(encoder, dt):
    return dt.isoformat()

@default.register(uuid.UUID)
def _(encoder, uuid_):
    return str(uuid_)
```

Serializing with both `json` and `simplejson` produce the same result:

```python
json.dumps({'datetime': datetime.now(), 'uuid': uuid.uuid4()})
# '{"datetime": "2018-01-28T19:24:51.343936", "uuid": "51571cbe-9462-41ab-be6a-5002aad1d6e3"}'
simplejson.dumps({'datetime': datetime.now(), 'uuid': uuid.uuid4()})
# '{"datetime": "2018-01-28T19:25:35.301389", "uuid": "323f2138-00d3-4ce8-81d0-2f4b19706319"}'
```

Passing in a `default` argument still works as expected:

```python
def explode(obj):
    raise NotImplementedError()

json.dumps({'datetime': datetime.now(), 'uuid': uuid.uuid4()}, default=explode)
# Traceback (most recent call last):
#   File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
#   File ".../python3.6/json/__init__.py", line 238, in dumps
#     **kw).encode(obj)
#   File ".../python3.6/json/encoder.py", line 199, in encode
#     chunks = self.iterencode(o, _one_shot=True)
#   File ".../python3.6/json/encoder.py", line 257, in iterencode
#     return _iterencode(o, 0)
#   File "<stdin>", line 2, in explode
# NotImplementedError

simplejson.dumps({'datetime': datetime.now(), 'uuid': uuid.uuid4()}, default=explode)
# Traceback (most recent call last):
#   File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
#   File ".../simplejson/__init__.py", line 399, in dumps
#     **kw).encode(obj)
#   File ".../simplejson/encoder.py", line 387, in encode
#     chunks = list(chunks)
#   File ".../simplejson/encoder.py", line 772, in _iterencode
#     for chunk in _iterencode_dict(o, _current_indent_level):
#   File ".../simplejson/encoder.py", line 729, in _iterencode_dict
#     for chunk in chunks:
#   File ".../simplejson/encoder.py", line 792, in _iterencode
#     o = _default(o)
#   File "<stdin>", line 2, in explode
# NotImplementedError
```

And subclassing still works as expected:

```python
class HalfDoneJSONEncoder(json.JSONEncoder):
    def default(self, o):
        if isinstance(o, uuid.UUID):
            return str(o)
        else:
            raise TypeError('Object of type %s is not JSON serializable' % o.__class__.__name__)

HalfDoneJSONEncoder().encode({'datetime': datetime.now(), 'uuid': uuid.uuid4()})
# Traceback (most recent call last):
#   File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
#   File ".../python3.6/json/encoder.py", line 199, in encode
#     chunks = self.iterencode(o, _one_shot=True)
#   File ".../python3.6/json/encoder.py", line 257, in iterencode
#     return _iterencode(o, 0)
#   File "<stdin>", line 6, in default
# TypeError: Object of type datetime is not JSON serializable

class HalfDoneSimpleJSONEncoder(simplejson.JSONEncoder):
    def default(self, o):
        if isinstance(o, datetime):
            return o.isoformat()
        else:
            raise TypeError('Object of type %s is not JSON serializable' % o.__class__.__name__)

HalfDoneSimpleJSONEncoder().encode({'datetime': datetime.now(), 'uuid': uuid.uuid4()})
# Traceback (most recent call last):
#   File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
#   File ".../simplejson/encoder.py", line 387, in encode
#     chunks = list(chunks)
#   File ".../simplejson/encoder.py", line 772, in _iterencode
#     for chunk in _iterencode_dict(o, _current_indent_level):
#   File ".../simplejson/encoder.py", line 729, in _iterencode_dict
#     for chunk in chunks:
#   File ".../simplejson/encoder.py", line 792, in _iterencode
#     o = _default(o)
#   File "<stdin>", line 6, in default
# TypeError: Object of type UUID is not JSON serializable
```

So it is absolutely possible to support both already.

> I think the way forward for this proposal is to implement a separate package that provides a factory for, and maybe even a default singleton instance of, such a registry. This way, it can be passed down to either json or simplejson and would be expected to work correctly in either case. You can even provide a wrapper methods for loads/dumps that dispatch to whichever library is preferred to save people the trouble of explicitly passing in the default argument.

I think this would be all that's necessary:

```python
def singledispatchmethod(...):
    ...

@singledispatchmethod
def default(encoder, obj):
    raise TypeError('Object of type %s is not JSON serializable' % obj.__class__.__name__)

import json
try:
    import simplejson
except ImportError:
    pass
else:
    simplejson.JSONEncoder.default = default

json.JSONEncoder.default = default
```
