Yes, I understand this, but if a requested resource cannot be played, eg. when it's blocked or geo-restricted, Streamlink should return an error message and exit with an error status code. Playing a static error video in these cases is not the job of the plugin, as it would make Streamlink return a different resource and would make it exit with a success code instead. This would not only be inconsistent, but also an incorrect behavior. That's why you have to write a wrapper script, so you can extend Streamlink with your needs.

----

*Sí, lo entiendo, pero si un recurso solicitado no se puede reproducir, por ejemplo, cuando está bloqueado o con restricciones geográficas, Streamlink debe devolver un mensaje de error y salir con un código de estado de error. Reproducir un vídeo de error estático en estos casos no es tarea del plugin, ya que haría que Streamlink devolviera un recurso diferente y lo haría salir con un código de éxito en su lugar. Esto no sólo sería inconsistente, sino también un comportamiento incorrecto. Es por eso que tienes que escribir un script de envoltura, para que puedas ampliar Streamlink con tus necesidades.*

It appears to me the root access here is for installing the required system packages. If you do the bootstrapping with the following steps it stays easy for inexperienced user while giving options to the experienced one:

- Check if required system packages are not installed.
- If thats the case notify the user - "Packages x,y,z need to be installed"
- Ask the user, if the script should install these packages
- If yes: Call the package manager with root permissions (call su / sudo only here)
- If no: Exit script with message "Please install the needed packages with your favorite package manager before executing this script again"

@joohoi, so I actually don't know of a case where the problem occurs, but it is possible according to the Apache documentation I linked above. To quote the documentation of graceful restart (which has the same effect of calling the init script with `reload`):

>When you issue a restart, a syntax check is first run, to ensure that there are no errors in the configuration files. If your configuration file has errors in it, you will get an error message about that syntax error, and the server will refuse to restart. This avoids the situation where the server halts and then cannot restart, leaving you with a non-functioning server.

>This still will not guarantee that the server will restart correctly.

With that said, it appears `restart` has the same behavior and we currently don't do anything to protect against this case.

Perhaps we only have to do polling when setting up challenges. While not failproof, we could configure an additional vhost in the call to `perform`. We can then attempt to poll this vhost a fixed number of times. If we receive the expected response, we continue.

If we never receive the correct response, I think the best behavior is to attempt to continue here as well. The reason is it is possible we're unable to connect to ourselves for whatever reason but Apache has successfully loaded the new config.

We'll also want to think about this: https://bugs.python.org/issue32221

tl;dr: in some versions of python, `getsockname` (etc.) on IPv6 addresses resolves any scopeid and appends it to the first element of the tuple, but this turns out to be super slow (and this is on a code path that gets called in `recvfrom` for UDP packets), so they switched to not doing this. The scopeid information is still available at the end of the 4-tuple, but at the least this means cross-version differences in how to interpret the 0th element in the tuple.

This also caused issues for twisted: https://twistedmatrix.com/trac/ticket/9449#9449

If the rescale has this function then I am not directly in favour of removing it. I see the preprocessing as something that only helps the subpx refinement; the characterization should - in my opinion - be about the non-processed image.

So I propose changing 'mass' to just the sum of raw px intensities. `signal` is probably better from the preprocessed image (currently it is taken from the raw image). `size` and `ecc` should be investigated.

I think that Savin & Doyle intended 'signal' to come from the unpreprocessed image; if they explicitly remove the background B in eq. (42), S_tot must be the raw image and not the preprocessed one. Also, they assume that noise averages out over the mask region, that's why in the denominator in there is no dS term: it integrates to zero. By the way, this denominator is the total sum of pixel intensities: no need to deal with `signal` at all, just use a background-corrected `mass`, which is a more robust quantity.

@stefanv, @jni, @rgommers 
I thought about this some more and I think using `cval` in the interpolation is technically correct for warping to coordinates outside the image range. As a second point of agreement, I get the same result as in scikit.image's linear interpolation when using OpenCV's remap function:

```python
import numpy as np
import cv2
img = np.ones((7, 7))
img[2:5, 2:5] = 0.0
scale_factor = 2.0
output_shape = scale_factor * np.asarray(img.shape)
# destination coordinates
axis_slices = tuple([slice(d) for d in output_shape])
nd_coords = np.mgrid[axis_slices]
map2_coord_arrays = [1/scale_factor * (nd_coords[i] + 0.5) - 0.5
                     for i, d in enumerate(output_shape)]
cv2_map = np.concatenate((map2_coord_arrays[0][..., np.newaxis],
                          map2_coord_arrays[1][..., np.newaxis]), axis=2)
img_cv2_remap = cv2.remap(img,
                          map1=cv2_map.astype(np.float32),
                          map2=None,
                          interpolation=cv2.INTER_LINEAR,
                          borderMode=cv2.BORDER_CONSTANT,
                          borderValue=0.)
f, ax = plt.subplots(1, 2)
ax[0].imshow(img)
ax[0].set_title('original')
ax[1].imshow(img_cv2_remap)
ax[1].set_title('opencv remap')
```
![opencv_result](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6528957/7779580/be198702-00a3-11e5-80ad-7eb40457e5d7.png)


Perhaps a simple solution to avoid potentially confusing edge results like the above during simple rescalings would be to change the default `mode` for `resize` and `rescale` to `reflect`.  In that mode, regardless which underlying interpolation is used, the results should be the same.
The result for 'reflect' intuitively looks more like one would expect for upscaling the source by two:
![opencv_reflect](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6528957/7779668/73f3f620-00a4-11e5-815b-aee114e1e908.png)

As another OpenCV comparison:
OpenCV has a resize() function, which does not have an option to specify the border behavior.  However, in practice it seems that for most interpolation types, any coordinates that fall outside the image range get clipped to the source coordinate range  (e.g. see here (where `sx` is the floor of the destination x coordinate and gets clipped to [0, width -1] for most interpolation types)
https://github.com/Itseez/opencv/blob/8e392251baf353c7bbcf23d3c96a9f18dde3230d/modules/imgproc/src/imgwarp.cpp#L3392-3405
In practice I think that should be equivalent to `mode='reflect'`

Ron, in python, all threads will launch on one core.  `ProcessPool` launches processes, and that's multi-core.  What I'm saying is that I typically run a hierarchical map structure, with one `ThreadPool` and one `ProcessPool`.  For example, using multiple threads in an inner `map` function, where an outer `map` function launches each invocation of the former in a new process.  Use threads where you need parallel workflow, but not heaving lifting... and processes for when you need heavier lifting.

As far as working around it... I don't know.  You are welcome to try, and if you discover a good work-around, please feel free to share your solution.

I'm afraid the LDAP protocol only allows full equality lookups on distinguished names:

https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc4517#section-3.3.9
https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc4517#section-4.2.15
>    The distinguishedNameMatch rule is an equality matching rule.

You may be able to perform your lookups on the actual field used to build the distinguished name, e.g:

```q = LdapGroup.objects.exclude(name__icontains="Fiscal")```

So I think this one's a bit of a gray area: `objects.delete` *itself* is synchronous; the problem is that other operations may not immediately see those updates. In particular, `objects.delete` followed by `buckets.list` seems to fall into the second bullet here:
  https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/consistency#eventually_consistent_operations

I don't know what the right answer is here; a few ideas:

1. Before we delete the object, we could first find out what bucket it's in, and poll in `_api.objects_delete` to make sure the object has also disappeared from the bucket listing.

2. We could *optionally* poll, say controlled by an argument to `Item.delete`. (There's plenty of room to bikeshed over the default value for that arg. 😁 )

3. We could just warn the user about this in the docs, and update the test.

These basically go in decreasing order of potential slowdown for the user, but also in increasing order of potential for user confusion.

I’ve been trying a bunch of different approaches to making this work in Travis so we can test with Python 3.

We rely on system-level Python packages like GDAL and Cairo, while Travis switches Python versions using virtualenv with `system-site-packages` turned off by default. Worse, the virtualenv `python` and `pip` executables in the Travis path are different from the system-level ones in `sudo`’s path called by `chef`.

I started out by taking [Armin’s Ronacher’s advice on skipping straight to 3.3](http://lucumr.pocoo.org/2013/5/21/porting-to-python-3-redux/), and experimented with a few methods for shimming a system path into the virtualenv artificially with [.pth files](https://docs.python.org/2/library/site.html):

```
install:
  - for DIR in /home/travis/virtualenv/python3*/lib/*/site-packages; do echo '/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages' >> $DIR/system.pth; echo '/usr/local/lib/python3/dist-packages' >> $DIR/system.pth; done
  - for DIR in /home/travis/virtualenv/python*/lib/*/site-packages; do echo '/usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages' >> $DIR/system.pth; echo '/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages' >> $DIR/system.pth; done
```

This worked to a degree, but [`_gdal.so` wasn’t compatible](https://travis-ci.org/openaddresses/machine/jobs/48142155) with Python 3. I added explicit `apt-get` installations of packages like `python3-gdal`, and did the pip install from Travis directly so that packages like `future` would be accessible in the virtualenv. The [compiled `_gdal` module was no longer available](https://travis-ci.org/openaddresses/machine/jobs/48180046) at all, and I’m unsure why.

I’ve now removed the path fiddling, and switched from Python 3.3 to Python 3.2 so I can take advantage of the [explicit availability of system site packages](https://github.com/travis-ci/travis-ci/issues/2219#issuecomment-41804942) in 3.2. We are much further along, breaking on the incompatibility of `unicodecsv` with Python 3 rather than GDAL:

https://travis-ci.org/openaddresses/machine/jobs/48180760

I’m working on the `ditch-node-py3` branch and squashing certain noisy commits.
`latest` tags are in: https://hub.docker.com/r/openaddr/machine/tags/

Advanced Mode functionality has been long merged into librealsense core, including v2.10.4.
No additional cmake flags are needed to enable it.

Note that version 2.10.4 is rather outdated. Sticking with it may prevent you from updating the device firmware, as well as taking advantage of multiple improvements and bug fixes that were introduced into librealsense in the last six months.
The center is aligned to the LEFT imager (as seen from the camera, so it's left eye).

The intrinsic parameters are calculated the same for depth.
For color librealsense adjusts the focal length and principal point in case of 4:3 aspect ratio, which is not done in DCM, and the rest is the same.
The distortion parameters are the same for both libraries, only that librealsense provides them as: 
`float coeffs[5]`
and DCM provides them as: 
`tangential distortion = {coeffs[2], coeffs[3]}` 
`radial distortion = {coeffs[0], coeefs[1], coeffs[4]}`
but the data is supposed to be the same.
If you don't use 4:3 aspect ratio, do you get different numbers?

I might be missing something that has been discussed offline or somewhere else but completely erasing interfaces sounds a bit drastic. Making them more similar to normal layers might be good, but it should be carefully thought out so that it doesn't muddy the waters. I think some separation between concepts are good to keep them separated in your head.

What sparked the idea of making interfaces a "multi-flag" entity was the proposal from @johnsca, in the first post, that flags could be implemented using specialized classes instead of limiting them to simple on/off toggles. I think that is a great idea since it would make it possible to create abstractions of complex logic (such as relations) while still maintaining the same flag handling using the reactive decorators.

I'm not convinced that the interfaces should hide the fact that there are multiple relations connected in the background. I agree that the relational data should be handled separately so that the data is exposed to the charm in a unified manner. What I don't think should be hidden is the state that each relation is in, hence my proposal.

Regarding copy&paste of this file. I think the current build tools give us no other choice than doing that.
Take for example the `greenlet` package. It ships a `greenlet.h` having similar content like ExtensionClass to access the C-code.

If I install this in a virtualenv, pip places the file in the following path `<virtualenv location>/include/site/python2.7/greenlet/greenlet.h`.
So far so good, but when another C-extension needs this header file, this include path is not added.
In the end something like this is called
```
x86_64-pc-linux-gnu-gcc -pthread -fPIC -I/usr/include/python2.7 -c test.c -o build/temp.linux-x86_64-2.7/test.o
test.c:1:22: fatal error: greenlet.h: No such file or directory
 #include "greenlet.h"
```

I have no idea what buildout does with header files of an egg.

Linux distributions solve this by moving the header files close the cpython header files.
Look for example here: https://packages.debian.org/jessie/amd64/python-greenlet-dev/filelist
Then it is possible to include them via `#include <greenlet/greenlet.h>`

So for a proper solution we need help from the sandbox.

I considered adding `__parent__` to an interface, but in the end it didn't seem worth it to canonicalize it like that.  There were two options, either (1) add it to `IAnnotations` or (2) create a subclass of `IAnnotations`, say `IAttributeAnnotations` that adds `__parent__` and whith `zope.annotation.attribute.AttributeAnnotation` now implements.

Adding it to `IAnnotations` would promote what I see as an implementation detail into the general interface. There might be other `IAnnotations` implementations out there that do not have a `__parent__` and they would now be out of compliance with the interface (for example, I could imagine an implementation for external data, like that stored in LDAP, that you get with something like `ldap_annotation = ldap_annotations.get('cn=foo,ou=bar,o=baz')` which wouldn't meaningfully have a `__parent__`).

Using a new subclass wouldn't have that problem but maybe it introduces its own compatibility issues (I don't know what they would be, but it seems like there might be some)? For the kind of [code I was concerned with](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.keyreference/blob/master/src/zope/keyreference/persistent.py#L138), that doesn't check interfaces or use `ILocation` or anything like that, it wouldn't really add any benefit, either. This is just helping this object comply with an ad-hoc protocol of having a `__parent__` chain when possible.

I don't feel super-strongly about that, though, so if anyone *does* feel strongly like this should be added to an interface, I can do that.

from io import BytesIO
from PIL import Image
from sklearn import datasets
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt


digits = datasets.load_digits()
frames = digits.images

slider = widgets.IntSlider(min=0, max=len(frames)-1, step=1)

#plot the image to bytebuffer
buf = BytesIO()
plt.imshow(frames[0], interpolation='nearest')
plt.savefig(buf, format='png')

image_widget = widgets.Image(
    value=buf.getvalue(),
    format='png',
    height = '500px',
    width = '300px'
)

main_layout = widgets.VBox([slider,image_widget])
display(main_layout)

# slider handler
def on_value_change(change):
    i = change['new']
    
    #plot the image to bytebuffer
    buf = BytesIO()
    plt.imshow(frames[i], interpolation='nearest')
    plt.savefig(buf, format='png')
    image_widget.value = buf.getvalue()
    
slider.observe(on_value_change, names='value')
```

The content of **Adobe Cmaps** is kept up-to-date in the [cmap-resources](https://github.com/adobe-type-tools/cmap-resources/) repository, and the following two patterns can be used to load only the ones that are necessary for CID-keyed OpenType/CFF font development:

```
*/CMap/*UTF32-H
*/CMap/*[35BC]p[cv]*-H
```

The above resolves to:

```
Adobe-CNS1-7/CMap/B5pc-H
Adobe-CNS1-7/CMap/UniCNS-UTF32-H
Adobe-GB1-5/CMap/GBpc-EUC-H
Adobe-GB1-5/CMap/UniGB-UTF32-H
Adobe-Japan1-6/CMap/83pv-RKSJ-H
Adobe-Japan1-6/CMap/UniJIS-UTF32-H
Adobe-Japan1-6/CMap/UniJIS2004-UTF32-H
Adobe-Japan1-6/CMap/UniJISX0213-UTF32-H
Adobe-Japan1-6/CMap/UniJISX02132004-UTF32-H
Adobe-Korea1-2/CMap/KSCpc-EUC-H
Adobe-Korea1-2/CMap/UniKS-UTF32-H
```

The content of **CID charsets** is a bit complex in that almost all of the files are not actually used by public AFDKO tools, but rather by a non-public tool that is used to compile CIDFont resources from a series of Type 1 (PFA) row fonts. Let me ponder this.

Woah. This is quite a serious change in architecture.

I would like to encourage that we move towards such changes with caution - it's a great idea to have optional compiled libraries, but it can seriously disrupt the downstream distributions of Kolibri.

One thing that I would like to suggest is to implement this with much less impact on the existing codebase. Not that I want to protect it from some great evil, but simply because Kolibri could *potentially* continue to be pure-python, while the compiled optimizations reside externally as a pluggable feature.

```
try:
     import quick_compiled_library
except ImportError:
    # fallback
```

On Linux-based systems, cryptography is already packaged. So it's possibly better to rely on this, since it's an optional improvement. For instance, that would give you [ARM packages for Raspberry Pi](http://archive.raspbian.org/raspbian/pool/main/p/python-cryptography/).

@66eli77 I would rather do this by extracting the content tracking behaviour into the learn content page, rather than adding a big conditional flag to the content-renderer itself. I have done this similarly for all the assessment related behaviour, where all the events like `answerGiven` etc. are propagated out of the content renderer and not handled by it itself.

So, basically, you would add methods to handle the `startTracking`, `stopTracking`, and `updateProgress` events that are currently being handled either by actions or by methods that call actions.

Instead they should propagate these events upwards so that the learn content page can listen to them, and we move the two actions and the method that calls an action to there.

See here for an example: https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/blob/develop/kolibri/core/assets/src/views/content-renderer/index.vue#L137

We have a similar requirement,
here is an optional solution, if approved I'll try and put up a PR:
1.  Support specifying the list of fields to be serialized in a nested schema using the dot notation in
the "only" argument. 
2. Add "only" to dumps/dump methods that overrides the fields and self.only
3. Raise validation error if the fields or nested fields passed as "only" aren't a subset
of Meta class fields.

`only` and `exclude` support dot delimited nested keys. `load_only` and `dump_only` (or `data_key` for v3) could too. There is another bug in 2.x that would prevent using the same key for the key for the list in the data and the schema. Adding this feature to v3 would work though. The implementation should be significantly simpler than it was for `only` and `exclude`.

```py
class ASchema(Schema):
    field = fields.Integer()

class BSchema(Schema):
    a = fields.Nested(ASchema)
    a2 = fields.List(fields.Nested(ASchema), data_key='a2.items')
```

The rationale for _validate_missing is as follows:
Consider a web API using a nested schema in which  both inner and outer fields are required, so valid JSON data looks like (e.g.) {outerRequired: {innerRequired: true}}.

Now suppose an API client submits a request containing valid data for
the outer schema, but missing the key for the inner schema, like
{outerRequired: {extra: true}}. Clearly, the client should be informed
that the *inner* schema was missing a required field. That was how
Marshmallow worked before #235.

But what if the client submits a request, say {extra: true}, that
doesn't have a key for the *outer* field? Before #235, the client would
have to submit an additional request just to discover the inner
required field. After #235, the client can be informed in a single
response of *everything* missing from the request data, now matter how
many levels deep.

In short, _validate_missing is an aid to discoverability. I hope that
clears things up for you.

Now the schemas is cleaner, but we need to provide -- for extensions like sphinx or swagger -- a simple way to introspect the schema defined for a view (method+content-type...).

If we want to make schema validation pluggable with any library (jsonschema, marshmallow, cerberus, colander, ...) then ideally we need to make schema introspection agnostic too.

BTW the method `service.schema_for(...)` is still there. 

A proposition would be to leave the `schema` argument, make it accept a standard pivot format like JSONSchema as dict.

```py
from cornice.validators import jsonschema_validator

person_schema = {"title": "Person", "fields": [...]}

service = ...

@service.post(schema=person_schema, validators=(jsonschema_validator(person_schema),))
def service_post(request):
....
```

This way, everything is explicit and easily introspectable. What do you think ?

The (fixed) old algorithm keeps more samples than your new one, so your new code is doing a better job at thinning for a given relative accuracy. As a fast workaround I wrote a custom wrapper around requests to pretend to be httplib2.

```python
class CustomHttp(object):
  def __init__(self, timeout=None):
    self.timeout = timeout

  def request(self, uri, method='GET', body=None, headers=None,
              redirections=None, connection_type=None):
    if connection_type is not None:
      uri = '%s://%s' % (connection_type, uri)
    resp = requests.request(method=method, url=uri, data=body, headers=headers,
                            timeout=self.timeout)
    resp.status = resp.status_code
    return resp, resp.content

credentials.authorize(CustomHttp())
```

@Downchuck It seems you are having an issue with [namespace packages][1] in your install.

The real problem is that `google` is a namespace that many other packages outside of our purview own. And if each of those packages tried to over-write `google/__init__.py`, bad things would happen.

There **should not** be a `google/__init__.py` file, but there **should** be `.pth` files, e.g.

```
$ ls -1 site-packages/*pth
site-packages/googleapis_common_protos-1.5.3-py3.6-nspkg.pth
site-packages/google_auth-1.1.1-py2.7-nspkg.pth
site-packages/google_cloud_core-0.27.1-py3.6-nspkg.pth
site-packages/google_cloud_storage-1.4.0-py3.6-nspkg.pth
site-packages/google_resumable_media-0.3.0-py3.6-nspkg.pth
site-packages/protobuf-3.4.0-py3.6-nspkg.pth
site-packages/pytest-cov.pth
```

[1]: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0420/

Looking at the errors above, I believe the problem here is that it appears that `DSX` specifies a custom build directory for `pip`. `pip` doesn't actively manage custom build directories, since those are usually used for incremental build optimizations. As such, that responsibility becomes the burden of the user, which in this case is `DSX`. I assume running `!pip install --upgrade pip` and restarting the kernel has triggered the deletion of those custom build directories, which is probably why you didn't see the error above anymore.

It may be better to explicitly specify that the generated code can be distributed under any license, similar to how [GCC does this](https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/libstdc++/manual/license.html).

A typical use case here would be to generate code, put that into a repository, add other code, maybe modify the generated code, and then distribute the whole product. If the generated code has its own Apache license, then the user needs to take care to document that (as required by the license), and you can end up in a situation where there is code with different licenses in the same repository (e.g. if another part of the code is GPL), which is always difficult to manage.

If the authors of schema salad don't care about being attributed every time someone uses --codegen to make some code to put into their project, then having an exception akin to the GCC one linked above is probably better, as it will keep less careful developers (and the majority of developers are not very careful when it comes to licensing) from accidentally violating a license on technical grounds.
